Right-edge column of file-style shortcuts for system windows. State-earned — a folder icon only appears once its predicate passes.

import LivePreview from "~/components/LivePreview.astro";

`DesktopIcons` renders a column of file-style shortcuts on the right edge of the desktop. Each shortcut maps to a system window whose `appearsAsDesktopIcon` predicate currently returns `true`. The library subscribes to storage change events so the column updates without polling.

<LivePreview label="Add an entry from Hello to make the Recents folder appear" height="420px" />

## Import

```tsx
import {
  DesktopIcons,
  registerSystemWindow,
  type SystemWindowDef,
} from "@react-ui-os/desktop";
```

## Props

None. The component reads from the registered system window list and the active storage adapter.

## Registering a system window

```tsx
registerSystemWindow("downloads", {
  name: "Downloads",
  accent: "#7c66f5",
  defaultBounds: { w: 560, h: 420 },
  content: DownloadsFolder,
  appearsAsDesktopIcon: (storage) => hasDownloads(storage),
});
```

The fields that control desktop-icon behavior:

| Field                   | Type                                                                            | Description                                                                                                                |
| ----------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `appearsAsDesktopIcon`  | `boolean \| ((storage: StorageAdapter) => boolean)`                              | `false` (default) hides the icon. `true` always shows it. A function is the **state-earned** pattern: evaluated on each storage change. |
| `desktopIcon`           | `ComponentType<{ size?: number }>`                                              | The icon painted on the shortcut. Defaults to a generic folder SVG.                                                        |

## State-earned folders

The library follows the rule: a folder icon should not exist until the user has put something in it. Same shape Mintables uses for Downloads and Presets:

```tsx
registerSystemWindow("downloads", {
  name: "Downloads",
  defaultBounds: { w: 560, h: 420 },
  content: DownloadsFolder,
  appearsAsDesktopIcon: (storage) => listDownloads(storage).length > 0,
});
```

- The first time `recordDownload(...)` writes to storage, the icon fades in.
- When the last entry is deleted, it fades out.
- The desktop never shows an empty-state pane for it, because the surface whose empty state you would design isn't there yet.

## Click behavior

| Gesture        | Effect                                                                       |
| -------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Single click   | Visual press (placeholder for a desktop selection model)                     |
| Double click   | Opens the matching system window via `openWindow({ kind: "system", systemId })` |

## Position

`DesktopIcons` reserves the upper-right edge, below the menu bar when present. Stacked vertically with 18 px gaps between tiles.

- [FileExplorer](/components/fileexplorer/) is the typical body for a folder-style system window.
- [`StorageAdapter`](/api/storageadapter/) is how the predicate reads state.
The floating app dock. Horizontal pill at the bottom or vertical rail on the left, depending on the active theme's chrome.

import LivePreview from "~/components/LivePreview.astro";

The dock renders one tile per registered app. Clicking a tile toggles the app's window: open if not running, focus + restore if minimized or unfocused, otherwise minimize. Hidden when `theme.chrome.dockPosition` is `"hidden"`.

<LivePreview label="The dock in action" height="380px" />

## Import

```tsx
import { Dock, DOCK_HEIGHT, DOCK_WIDTH, getDockTileRect } from "@react-ui-os/desktop";
```

## Props

None. The dock reads everything it needs from `useApps()` and `useTheme()`.

## Position

The dock honors `theme.chrome.dockPosition`:

| Value      | Layout                                                                                  |
| ---------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `"bottom"` | Horizontal pill centered at the bottom edge with hover-lift on `translateY`.            |
| `"left"`   | Vertical rail centered on the left edge with hover-lift on `translateX`.                |
| `"hidden"` | Returns `null`. Apps are reachable via Spotlight, keyboard shortcuts, or `openWindow`.  |

## Tile appearance

Each tile renders the app's accent as a linear gradient with a top sheen. The library accepts three optional visual hooks per app:

| Field            | Used by tile                                                                            |
| ---------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `app.iconArt`    | A subject illustration painted on top of the gradient. Best for distinctive identities. |
| `app.icon`       | A small Lucide-style icon component, fallback when `iconArt` is absent.                 |
| `app.name`       | Initial letter shown if neither icon is provided.                                       |

## Indicator dot

A small dot under (bottom dock) or beside (left dock) the tile reflects state:

- **Hidden** when the app is not open
- **Dim** when open but unfocused (or minimized)
- **Bright** (using `theme.palette.textPrimary`) when focused

## Helper: `getDockTileRect`

```tsx
import { getDockTileRect } from "@react-ui-os/desktop";

const rect = getDockTileRect("notes");
// → DOMRect | null
```

Returns the live bounding rect of the named dock tile. Used internally by the minimize-genie animation; consumers can use it to align tooltips, badges, or "fly-to-dock" effects.

## Exports

| Export         | Description                                                                                                          |
| -------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `DOCK_HEIGHT`  | Footprint when docked at the bottom (default 76 px). Useful for `position: fixed` layouts that need to clear it.     |
| `DOCK_WIDTH`   | Footprint when docked on the left. Same purpose.                                                                     |

## See also

- [MenuBar](/components/menubar/) sits on the opposite edge.
- [Themes overview](/themes/overview/) explains `chrome.dockPosition`.

import LivePreview from "~/components/LivePreview.astro";

`MenuBar` renders the top 28 px of the desktop. It is system chrome — application-specific actions belong inside the window, not here. Hidden when `theme.chrome.menuBar` is `"none"`.

<LivePreview label="The menu bar tracking the focused window" height="320px" />

## Import

```tsx
import { MenuBar, MENU_BAR_HEIGHT } from "@react-ui-os/desktop";
```

## Props

| Prop    | Type     | Default          | Description                                                       |
| ------- | -------- | ---------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `brand` | `string` | `"react-ui-os"`  | Label rendered on the far left of the bar in bold.                |

## Composition

```text
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ brand · focused-app-name                          Thu Mar 5 13:42 │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

- **Left cluster:** the `brand` prop, followed by the focused app's `name` (or the focused system window's resolved name) separated by a thin border.
- **Right cluster:** a live system clock formatted via `Intl.DateTimeFormat`. SSR-safe: renders nothing on first paint, then updates on mount.

## What goes here

✓ Brand label.
✓ Focused application name.
✓ System-level status: clock, online indicator, sync state.

✗ App-specific actions, menus, or buttons. Those belong inside the window.

## Configuration via theme

| `theme.chrome.menuBar` | Behavior                                                                                   |
| ---------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| `"top"`                | Renders the bar at the top of the viewport (default).                                      |
| `"none"`               | Returns `null`. App identity then needs to surface inside the windows themselves.          |

## Exports

| Export             | Description                                                                                |
| ------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| `MENU_BAR_HEIGHT`  | Pixel height of the bar (28). Useful when laying out custom surfaces that must clear it.   |

- [Themes overview](/themes/overview/) covers the `chrome.menuBar` token.
- [Window](/components/window/) reads `MENU_BAR_HEIGHT` to position its top edge.
A real desktop window (not a dialog) that reads the active theme's customizable schema and renders one editor per field.

import LivePreview from "~/components/LivePreview.astro";

`Settings` is a system window opened by `Cmd-,` (macOS convention), by typing "settings" into Spotlight, or programmatically via `openWindow({ kind: "system", systemId: "settings" })`. It reads `theme.customizable` and renders the appropriate editor per field kind.

<LivePreview label="Press Cmd-, to open Settings and tweak the theme live" height="420px" />

## Import

```tsx
import { Settings } from "@react-ui-os/desktop";
```

In normal use you never instantiate `<Settings>` directly. The library registers it as a system window automatically; opening it goes through the window manager.

## How it works

1. The active theme declares a `customizable` map: keys are dotted paths into the theme (e.g. `"palette.accent"`), values describe the field kind.
2. Opening Settings reads that schema and renders one row per entry, grouped by `section`.
3. Edits write to the storage adapter under `settings:<themeId>`.
4. The effective-theme overlay applies the change immediately; the rest of the desktop reads tokens via `useTheme()` and re-renders.
5. Reload restores the user's overrides via the same storage adapter.

## Supported field kinds

| Kind                  | Editor                                                                                      |
| --------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `color-from-palette`  | A row of color swatches. Selected swatch wears an outline.                                  |
| `image-pick`          | A thumbnail grid. Each option has an `src` and a `label`.                                   |
| `range`               | A slider with `min` / `max` / `step` and a tabular-numeric readout (optional `unit`).       |
| `select`              | A segmented control where each `option` has a `value` and a `label`.                        |
| `toggle`              | An iOS-style switch with a 36 × 20 rounded pill and a sliding thumb.                        |

## Reset buttons

The panel surfaces two reset buttons contextually:

- **Per-field Reset** appears next to any field whose value is currently overridden.
- **Reset all** appears in the header when at least one override exists.

Both write through `useSettings()` so any subscriber (the desktop itself, the dock, a custom inspector) re-renders.

## Programmatic access

```tsx
import { useSettings } from "@react-ui-os/desktop";

function MyCustomToolbar() {
  const { schema, prefs, setPref, resetAll } = useSettings();
  // …
}
```

See the [`useSettings`](/api/usesettings/) reference for the full hook surface.

## Accessibility

- The window inherits the standard `role="region" aria-label="Settings window"`.
- Toggle controls use `role="switch" aria-checked`.
- Color swatches expose the hex string via `aria-label`.

## See also

- [`useSettings`](/api/usesettings/) drives the panel.
- [Customizable schema](/themes/customizable/) explains how a theme declares its field set.
- [Themes overview](/themes/overview/) covers the broader theme model.
A Cmd-K command palette that fuzzy-searches across registered apps and system windows. Activating any result reuses the same openWindow primitive a dock click uses.

import LivePreview from "~/components/LivePreview.astro";

`Spotlight` is the keyboard front door. It is self-contained: owns its own open/close state, listens for both Cmd-K and a custom event, and restores the previously focused element when it closes.

<LivePreview label="Press Cmd-K (or Ctrl-K) inside the preview" height="420px" />

## Import

```tsx
import { Spotlight, SPOTLIGHT_OPEN_EVENT } from "@react-ui-os/desktop";
```

## Props

None. Drop the component anywhere inside `<DesktopProvider>` and it works. The default `<Desktop>` already mounts it.

## Opening Spotlight

| Trigger                                                         | Behavior                                                        |
| --------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `Cmd-K` (macOS) / `Ctrl-K` (elsewhere)                           | Toggles the palette. Bails when typing in an input or textarea. |
| `window.dispatchEvent(new CustomEvent(SPOTLIGHT_OPEN_EVENT))`    | Opens from any component without prop drilling.                 |
| A custom button calling the above                                | Standard pattern for a "search" button in your own menu bar.    |

## Result kinds

| Kind     | Source                                                                                  |
| -------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| App      | Every entry in the `apps` prop passed to `<Desktop>` / `<DesktopProvider>`.             |
| System   | Every entry in the `systemWindows` registry (Settings by default, plus any you register). |

Each row shows the app or system window's accent tile, name, and tagline. Activating an entry calls `openWindow(...)`, the same primitive a dock click uses. From "I typed three letters" to "the right window is on top" is one keystroke and one dispatch.

## Keyboard

| Key            | Effect                                                              |
| -------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| ↑ / ↓          | Move selection. Wraps at the ends.                                  |
| Enter          | Activate the selected result.                                       |
| Escape         | Dismiss. Focus returns to whatever owned it before Spotlight opened. |
| Cmd/Ctrl-K     | Dismiss (toggle off).                                               |

## Adding your own results

A future release exposes `registerSpotlightSource(...)` for arbitrary result kinds (docs pages, recent files, anything). For now, contribute via `registerSystemWindow(...)` — every registered system window automatically shows up as a Spotlight result.

## Accessibility

- Backdrop is `role="presentation"` with click-to-dismiss.
- Panel is `role="dialog" aria-modal="true" aria-label="Spotlight"`.
- Results list is `role="listbox" aria-label="Spotlight results"`.
- Each row is `role="option" aria-selected="<bool>"`.
- Focus restoration on close — never traps the user.

## See also

- [KeyboardShortcuts](/components/desktopicons/#see-also) registers the global Cmd-K binding.
- [registerSystemWindow](/components/desktopicons/) — how to surface custom windows in Spotlight.
A single draggable resizable window with macOS-style traffic lights, focus tracking, and minimize-to-dock genie animation.

import LivePreview from "~/components/LivePreview.astro";

You will rarely instantiate `<Window>` directly. The [WindowLayer](#see-also) compositor renders one per entry in the window manager's state, and the window manager opens them via `openWindow(...)`. The component is exported for the rare case where you compose your own layer.

<LivePreview label="Drag, resize, focus, minimize" height="380px" />

## Import

```tsx
import { Window } from "@react-ui-os/desktop";
import type { OpenWindow } from "@react-ui-os/core";
```

## Props

| Prop  | Type         | Description                                                                                                              |
| ----- | ------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| `win` | `OpenWindow` | The window manager's record for this instance. Carries id, payload (app or system), state, bounds, and z-index.          |

## Interaction model

| Gesture                                | Behavior                                                                                  |
| -------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Drag title bar                         | Moves the window. Transform written to DOM during the gesture; React state on `pointerup` |
| Double-click title bar                 | Toggles maximize / restore                                                                |
| Drag an edge                           | Resizes along that axis                                                                   |
| Drag a corner                          | Resizes along both axes                                                                   |
| Click traffic light: red               | Plays the close animation then dispatches `closeWindow(id)`                               |
| Click traffic light: yellow            | Plays the genie minimize toward the matching dock tile                                    |
| Click traffic light: green             | Toggles maximize                                                                          |
| Click anywhere on the window           | Focuses it (z-bumps and updates `focusedId`)                                              |
| Escape (when focused and maximized)    | Restores to the previous bounds                                                           |

## Animations

| Phase     | Duration                  | Easing                  |
| --------- | ------------------------- | ----------------------- |
| Open      | `theme.motion.windowOpenDurationMs` | `theme.motion.windowOpenEasing` |
| Close     | Same as open, reversed    | Same                    |
| Minimize  | `theme.motion.genieDurationMs`      | `theme.motion.genieEasing`      |

The minimize animation reads the matching dock tile's `DOMRect` at gesture time, sets two CSS custom properties (`--genie-dx`, `--genie-dy`) on the window element, and plays a keyframe that scales it toward those coordinates. Each window flies to its own dock tile.

## Performance

Drag and resize avoid React re-renders entirely during the gesture. The pointer handler reads bounds from a ref, writes `transform` directly to the DOM element, and only dispatches `setBounds` on `pointerup`. With four windows open and each containing a live preview, drags stay at 60 fps.

## Accessibility

- The root is a `<div role="region" aria-label="<Name> window">`.
- Traffic lights are `<button>` elements with `aria-label="Close"`, `"Minimize"`, `"Maximize"`.
- Title-bar drag is pointer-only; keyboard users move and resize windows via the shortcuts handled by [KeyboardShortcuts](/components/desktopicons/#see-also).

## See also

- [WindowLayer](/components/desktopicons/#see-also) renders the compositor.
- [useWindowManager](/api/usewindowmanager/) drives state transitions.

In Next.js App Router, mark the consuming module with `"use client"`: ```tsx title="app/page.tsx" "use client"; import { Desktop } from "@react-ui-os/desktop"; // ... ``` In Astro, mount the desktop as a React island with `client:load`: ```astro --- import Desktop from "../components/Desktop"; --- ``` Server-side, the desktop renders nothing (every JSX surface that touches `window` returns `null`), so hydration is safe. ## Troubleshooting **The dock appears but windows don't drag.** Your bundler may be stripping `"use client"` directives. What react-ui-os is, where it fits, and when to pick it.

react-ui-os is a React component library that ships a working OS-style desktop in one line. Drop in `<Desktop apps={apps} theme={theme} />` and your page is a desktop: wallpaper, dock, draggable windows with macOS-style traffic lights, Spotlight, Settings, a Finder-style file explorer.

## What's in the box

- **A window manager** with focus tracking, drag, resize, maximize, minimize-to-dock genie animation, stable per-app instance ids.
- **A theme system** with build-time switching and a runtime Settings panel for end-user tweaks (the active theme declares which tokens are user-customizable).
- **Spotlight**, a Cmd-K command palette that fuzzy-searches across registered apps and system windows.
- **Settings** as a real desktop window that renders the active theme's `customizable` schema.
- **A FileExplorer** primitive with the macOS Finder interaction model: multi-select, Shift-range, right-click context menu, F2 rename.
- **State-earned desktop folders** so the desktop icon for a folder only appears once the user has put something in it.
- **Two themes** out of the box: `theme-default` (unbranded baseline) and `theme-mintables` (cinematic frosted glass).

## When to reach for it

**Pick react-ui-os when:**

- The product *is* the desktop metaphor — a tool that lives in a browser tab but reads like an application (a creative app, a maker tool, an admin shell, a workshop).
- You want a coherent OS feeling instead of a webpage with chrome on top of it.
- Multi-window workflows matter (compare two views side by side, keep a reference open while editing).

**Don't pick react-ui-os when:**

- You're building a content site or a marketing page. Reach for shadcn/ui or Tailwind UI instead.
- You need form-heavy components (selects, date pickers, tables). The library has the desktop chrome; bring your own form primitives.
- You can't ship JavaScript. The desktop is a runtime; SSR is supported but the desktop boots client-side.

## Three depths of API

The same primitives are reachable at three levels.

```tsx
// 1. One-line desktop — full default composition.
<Desktop apps={apps} theme={defaultTheme} />

// 2. Composable provider — pick which surfaces to render.
<DesktopProvider apps={apps} theme={theme}>
  <Wallpaper />
  <MenuBar />
  <WindowLayer />
  <Dock />
  <Spotlight />
</DesktopProvider>

// 3. Hooks — drive the system from anywhere.
const { openWindow, focusedWindow } = useWindowManager();
const theme = useTheme();
```

The 80% case is depth 1. Step down only when you need to.

## Mintables

[Mintables](https://github.com/saschb2b/mintables) is a parametric 3D-part generator (tubes, adapters, dividers, leg caps for furniture) that built the original OS-style desktop the library generalized from. It uses the same window manager, Spotlight, Settings, and Finder-style FileExplorer to host its Downloads and Presets folders.

The library and Mintables share a heritage but are independent: Mintables predates the library, and the library distills the patterns that worked into something other products can reuse.

How a theme declares which of its tokens end users may tweak through Settings.

The `customizable` field on a theme is a map from **dotted theme paths** to **field descriptors**. The library renders one editor per entry inside the Settings system window, applies user overrides on top of the theme defaults, and persists them through the storage adapter.

## The map

```ts
customizable: {
  "palette.accent": {
    kind: "color-from-palette",
    label: "Accent",
    options: ["#5cb6b9", "#7c66f5", "#a855f7", "#ec4899"],
  },
  "shape.windowRadius": {
    kind: "range",
    label: "Window radius",
    min: 0,
    max: 24,
    step: 2,
    unit: "px",
  },
  "chrome.dockPosition": {
    kind: "select",
    label: "Dock position",
    options: [
      { value: "bottom", label: "Bottom" },
      { value: "left",   label: "Left" },
      { value: "hidden", label: "Hidden" },
    ],
  },
}
```

- **Keys are dotted paths** into `OsTheme`. The library walks them to write the override.
- A theme that omits `customizable` (or sets it to `{}`) exposes no Settings panel.
- Stale prefs (paths the theme later removed) are silently ignored.

## Field kinds

| Kind                  | Editor                                                                                  | Required fields                                            |
| --------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- |
| `color-from-palette`  | A row of color swatches.                                                                | `options: string[]` (hex / CSS colors)                     |
| `image-pick`          | A thumbnail grid.                                                                       | `options: Array<{ src: string; label: string }>`           |
| `range`               | A slider with a tabular-numeric readout.                                                | `min`, `max`, `step`; optional `unit`                      |
| `select`              | A segmented control.                                                                    | `options: Array<{ value: string; label: string }>`         |
| `toggle`              | An iOS-style switch (`role="switch"`).                                                  | _(none)_                                                   |

Every kind also accepts `label: string`, `description?: string`, and `section?: string`.

## Grouping with `section`

```ts
"palette.accent":        { section: "Appearance", … },
"shape.windowRadius":    { section: "Appearance", … },
"motion.genieDurationMs":{ section: "Motion", … },
"chrome.dockPosition":   { section: "Layout", … },
```

Fields sharing a `section` value render together with the section name as a small uppercase heading.

## Effective tokens

```text
defaults  <  declared theme  <  user prefs
```

At runtime the active theme is `applyPrefs(baseTheme, prefs)`. The function walks each pref path, shallow-clones along the way, and writes the leaf — leaving everything else untouched. The library re-runs this on every pref change.

## Reading what a theme exposes

From any component:

```tsx
import { useSettings } from "@react-ui-os/desktop";

const { schema } = useSettings();
const fieldsByCategory = Object.entries(schema).map(([path, def]) => ({
  path,
  category: path.split(".")[0],
  ...def,
}));
```

The full hook surface is in [`useSettings`](/api/usesettings/).

## See also

- [Settings](/components/settings/) — the UI that renders the schema.
- [`useSettings`](/api/usesettings/) — the read / write hook.
- [Writing a theme](/themes/writing/) — declaring `customizable` in your own theme.
The skeleton is one thing. The look is a theme. react-ui-os ships a neutral baseline plus a cinematic Mintables theme, and you can write your own.
